questions.question: Which data structure follows the Last In First Out (LIFO) principle?
questions.explanation: A Stack follows LIFO — the last element pushed is the first one popped, like a stack of plates.

questions.question: Which data structure follows the First In First Out (FIFO) principle?
questions.explanation: A Queue follows FIFO — elements are removed in the same order they were added, like a line of people.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element by index in an array?
questions.explanation: Array elements occupy contiguous memory, so any index can be reached directly in constant O(1) time.

questions.question: In a singly linked list, each node contains:
options: Data and a pointer to the next node
questions.explanation: Each node in a singly linked list stores a data value and a single pointer/reference to the next node.

questions.question: Which data structure is typically used to implement Breadth-First Search (BFS)?
questions.explanation: BFS uses a Queue to explore nodes level by level, ensuring nodes are visited in the order they are discovered.

questions.question: In a Binary Search Tree (BST), which property must hold for every node?
options: Left subtree values > node > right subtree values
Left subtree values < node < right subtree values
All nodes at the same level are equal
Right subtree values < node < left subtree values
questions.explanation: In a BST, all values in the left subtree are less than the node, and all values in the right subtree are greater.

questions.question: What is the height of a perfectly balanced binary tree with n nodes?
questions.explanation: A balanced binary tree has O(log n) height because each level doubles the node count, so the depth grows logarithmically.

questions.question: Which data structure is used internally to manage function call stacks in a program?
questions.explanation: The call stack uses a Stack to track function invocations, local variables, and return addresses in LIFO order.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of a single heapify-down operation on a binary heap with n elements?
questions.explanation: Heapify-down compares and swaps an element at most O(log n) times, proportional to the height of the heap.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of building a max-heap from an unsorted array of n elements using the bottom-up approach?
questions.explanation: Bottom-up heap construction runs in O(n) because most nodes are near the leaves and need little or no sifting.

questions.question: What is the amortized time complexity of a push operation on a dynamic array that doubles in size when full?
questions.explanation: Although resizing is O(n), it happens infrequently. Amortized over all pushes, the cost per push averages out to O(1).

questions.question: Which of the following is a key property of a Red-Black Tree?
options: Every path from root to leaf has the same number of red nodes
A red node cannot have a red child
Every node is colored red or blue
The root node must always be red
questions.explanation: Red-Black Trees forbid consecutive red nodes (no red parent–red child). This, combined with other rules, ensures O(log n) height.

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of search, insert, and delete in a balanced AVL tree?
questions.explanation: AVL trees maintain strict balance (heights differ by at most 1), guaranteeing O(log n) for all three operations.

questions.question: Which data structure provides the most efficient implementation of a priority queue?
questions.explanation: A Binary Heap supports O(log n) insert and O(log n) extract-min/max with O(1) peek, making it optimal for priority queues.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of n elements?
questions.explanation: Binary search halves the search space each step, giving O(log n) comparisons in the worst case.

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of bubble sort?
questions.explanation: Bubble sort compares each pair of adjacent elements repeatedly. In the worst case it performs O(n²) comparisons.

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm uses the divide-and-conquer strategy by splitting the array in half?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort divides the array into two halves, recursively sorts each, then merges them — a classic divide-and-conquer algorithm.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of linear search in the worst case?
questions.explanation: Linear search checks each element one by one. In the worst case (target not present or at the end), it scans all n elements.

questions.question: Which data structure does Depth-First Search (DFS) use?
questions.explanation: DFS uses a Stack (or the implicit call stack via recursion) to explore as deep as possible before backtracking.

questions.question: What is the best-case time complexity of QuickSort?
questions.explanation: When the pivot always splits the array into equal halves, QuickSort achieves O(n log n) — its best and average case.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes dynamic programming?
options: Always picks the locally optimal choice at each step
Solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results
Explores all possible solutions using backtracking
Divides a problem into independent subproblems
questions.explanation: Dynamic programming stores (memoizes) results of overlapping subproblems to avoid redundant computation, trading space for time.

questions.question: What is Dijkstra's algorithm used for?
options: Finding the shortest path in a weighted graph with non-negative edges
Detecting cycles in a directed graph
questions.explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a source to all other vertices in a graph with non-negative edge weights.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Merge Sort in all cases?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort always splits into halves and merges, giving O(n log n) in best, average, and worst cases.

questions.question: A greedy algorithm is best described as:
options: Breaking problems into subproblems and caching results
Making the locally optimal choice at each step hoping to reach a global optimum
Trying all possibilities and picking the best
questions.explanation: A greedy algorithm selects the best available option at each step without reconsidering previous choices, which works for some but not all problems.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the Floyd-Warshall all-pairs shortest path algorithm?
questions.explanation: Floyd-Warshall uses three nested loops over all vertices, resulting in O(V³) time complexity.

questions.question: What problem can Bellman-Ford solve that Dijkstra's algorithm cannot?
options: Single-source shortest path in dense graphs
Shortest path in graphs with negative edge weights
questions.explanation: Bellman-Ford handles graphs with negative edge weights (and detects negative cycles), while Dijkstra requires non-negative weights.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) string pattern matching algorithm for text of length n and pattern of length m?
questions.explanation: KMP preprocesses the pattern in O(m) and scans the text in O(n), giving a total time complexity of O(n + m).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)?
options: It can be solved in polynomial time using dynamic programming
It is NP-hard and the best exact algorithm runs in O(2ⁿ · n²)
It can be solved greedily in O(n log n)
It is solvable in O(n³) using Floyd-Warshall
questions.explanation: TSP is NP-hard. The Held-Karp dynamic programming algorithm solves it in O(2ⁿ · n²), which is still exponential.

questions.question: Which data structure, when used with Prim's MST algorithm, achieves a time complexity of O(E log V)?
questions.explanation: Using a binary heap (priority queue) for the cut edges reduces Prim's overall complexity from O(V²) to O(E log V).

It can be solved in polynomial time using dynamic programming
It is NP-hard and the best exact algorithm runs in O(2ⁿ · n²)
It can be solved greedily in O(n log n)

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the Floyd-Warshall all-pairs shortest path algorithm (Practice 11)?

questions.question: What does SQL stand for?
questions.explanation: SQL stands for Structured Query Language and is the standard language for interacting with relational databases.

questions.question: What is a primary key in a relational database?
options: A key that references another table
A column that can contain duplicate values
A unique identifier for each record in a table
The first column of every table
questions.explanation: A primary key is a column (or set of columns) that uniquely identifies each row in a table. It must be unique and not null.

questions.question: What is a foreign key?

A column that references the primary key of another table
A unique constraint on a column
questions.explanation: A foreign key is a column in one table that references the primary key of another table, establishing a relationship between them.

questions.question: What is the purpose of database normalization?
options: To increase data redundancy for faster reads
To organize data to reduce redundancy and improve integrity
To merge all tables into one
questions.explanation: Normalization organizes a database into well-structured tables to reduce data redundancy and prevent update/insert/delete anomalies.

questions.question: What does an INNER JOIN return?
options: All rows from the left table
All rows from both tables regardless of matches
Only the rows where there is a match in both tables
All rows from the right table
questions.explanation: INNER JOIN returns only the rows that have matching values in both joined tables based on the join condition.

questions.question: What is a database index used for?
options: Speeding up data retrieval on queried columns
questions.explanation: An index is a data structure (usually a B-tree) that allows the database to find rows faster without scanning the entire table.

questions.question: What does the 'A' in ACID properties of a transaction stand for?
questions.explanation: ACID stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. Atomicity means a transaction either fully completes or fully rolls back.

questions.question: What is the key difference between 2NF and 3NF?
options: 2NF removes multi-valued dependencies; 3NF removes join dependencies
2NF removes partial dependencies on the primary key; 3NF removes transitive dependencies
2NF allows transitive dependencies; 3NF removes partial dependencies
There is no practical difference between them
questions.explanation: 2NF eliminates partial dependencies (non-key attribute depends on part of a composite key). 3NF further eliminates transitive dependencies (non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute).

questions.question: Which data structure is typically used to implement a database index?
questions.explanation: Databases use B-trees or B+ trees for indexes because they remain balanced, support range queries, and minimize disk I/O with their high branching factor.

questions.question: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking?
options: Optimistic locking uses row locks; pessimistic uses table locks
Optimistic locking assumes conflicts are rare and checks at commit; pessimistic locks resources immediately
Pessimistic locking is faster; optimistic is slower
They are different names for the same mechanism
questions.explanation: Optimistic locking allows concurrent access and detects conflicts at commit time (using versioning). Pessimistic locking blocks other transactions immediately by acquiring locks.

questions.question: According to the CAP theorem, a distributed database can guarantee at most which two of three properties simultaneously?
questions.explanation: The CAP theorem states a distributed system can provide at most two of: Consistency (every read gets the latest write), Availability (every request gets a response), and Partition tolerance (system works despite network splits).

questions.question: What is a materialized view?
options: A virtual table defined by a query that is computed on every access
A precomputed query result stored physically on disk and refreshed periodically
A view that is read-only and cannot be updated
An indexed view available only in NoSQL databases
questions.explanation: A materialized view stores the result of a query physically, enabling faster reads at the cost of storage and needing periodic refresh when underlying data changes.

questions.question: What is a process in an operating system?
questions.explanation: A process is a program in execution. It includes the program code, current activity (PC, registers), and its memory space.

questions.question: What is a thread?
options: A separate process with its own memory space
A lightweight unit of execution within a process sharing the same memory
questions.explanation: A thread is the smallest unit of CPU scheduling within a process. Threads share the process's memory and resources but have their own stack and registers.

questions.question: What is a deadlock?
options: A situation where a process exhausts available memory
A situation where two or more processes wait indefinitely for resources held by each other
A type of disk read error
questions.explanation: Deadlock occurs when a set of processes are each waiting for a resource held by another process in the set, creating a circular wait with no way to proceed.

questions.question: What is virtual memory?
options: An abstraction that gives each process the illusion of a large, contiguous address space
A faster type of cache memory
Memory shared between all running processes
questions.explanation: Virtual memory abstracts physical memory, allowing each process to use more memory than physically available by using disk as an extension of RAM via paging.

questions.question: What happens during a context switch?
options: The operating system terminates the current process
The CPU saves the current process state and restores another process state
questions.explanation: During a context switch, the OS saves the CPU state (registers, PC) of the running process and loads the previously saved state of another process.

questions.question: What is a semaphore in operating systems used for?
options: Synchronizing access to shared resources between processes or threads
questions.explanation: A semaphore is a synchronization primitive (an integer counter) used to control access to shared resources and coordinate process execution.

questions.question: What is thrashing in the context of virtual memory?
options: A CPU executing too many threads simultaneously
Excessive paging activity where the OS spends more time swapping pages than executing processes
A type of file system corruption
questions.explanation: Thrashing occurs when a system has too little physical memory, causing the OS to constantly page in and page out data, severely degrading performance.

questions.question: How does the Round Robin CPU scheduling algorithm work?
options: Highest priority process always runs first
Processes run to completion before the next one starts
Each process is given an equal fixed time quantum in cyclic order
Shortest job is always executed next
questions.explanation: Round Robin gives each process a fixed time slice (quantum) in cyclic order. If a process does not finish in its quantum, it is preempted and placed at the back of the queue.

questions.question: What is a page fault?
options: When a process accesses a page that is not currently in physical memory
An attempt to write to a read-only file
An overflow of the process stack
questions.explanation: A page fault occurs when a process tries to access a virtual memory page that the OS has not yet loaded into physical RAM, triggering the OS to load it from disk.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a mutex and a semaphore?
options: A mutex allows multiple threads to enter; a semaphore allows only one
A mutex is owned by the thread that locks it and only that thread can unlock it; a semaphore has no ownership
A mutex is used for inter-process communication; a semaphore is only for threads
questions.explanation: A mutex (mutual exclusion lock) has ownership — only the thread that acquired it can release it. A semaphore is a signaling mechanism with no ownership, allowing any thread to signal it.

questions.question: What is a semaphore in operating systems used for (Practice 6)?
options: Synchronizing access to shared resources between processes or threads
questions.explanation: A semaphore is a synchronization primitive (an integer counter) used to control access to shared resources and coordinate process execution. (Practice variant.)

questions.question: What is thrashing in the context of virtual memory (Practice 7)?
options: Excessive paging activity where the OS spends more time swapping pages than executing processes
A type of file system corruption
A CPU executing too many threads simultaneously
questions.explanation: Thrashing occurs when a system has too little physical memory, causing the OS to constantly page in and page out data, severely degrading performance. (Practice variant.)

questions.question: How does the Round Robin CPU scheduling algorithm work (Practice 8)?
options: Processes run to completion before the next one starts
Each process is given an equal fixed time quantum in cyclic order
Shortest job is always executed next
Highest priority process always runs first
questions.explanation: Round Robin gives each process a fixed time slice (quantum) in cyclic order. If a process does not finish in its quantum, it is preempted and placed at the back of the queue. (Practice variant.)

questions.question: What is a page fault (Practice 9)?
options: An overflow of the process stack
When a process accesses a page that is not currently in physical memory
An attempt to write to a read-only file
questions.explanation: A page fault occurs when a process tries to access a virtual memory page that the OS has not yet loaded into physical RAM, triggering the OS to load it from disk. (Practice variant.)

questions.question: What is the Banker's Algorithm used for?
options: Disk scheduling to minimize seek time
Deadlock avoidance by checking if resource allocation leads to a safe state
questions.explanation: The Banker's Algorithm is a deadlock avoidance algorithm. Before granting resources, it simulates allocation and checks whether the system remains in a safe state.

questions.question: Which page replacement algorithm replaces the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future?
questions.explanation: The Optimal (OPT) algorithm achieves the lowest page fault rate by replacing the page that will not be used for the longest future time, though it requires future knowledge.

questions.question: What is Belady's Anomaly?
options: An LRU algorithm bug causing extra page faults
A phenomenon where increasing the number of page frames increases page faults in FIFO
A deadlock caused by incorrect semaphore usage
questions.explanation: Belady's Anomaly is the counter-intuitive phenomenon where allocating more page frames to a process using FIFO page replacement can actually increase the number of page faults.

questions.question: What is a spinlock?
options: A lock that puts the waiting thread to sleep
A type of semaphore counting resources
A busy-wait lock where the thread continuously polls until the lock is free
A lock used exclusively in user space
questions.explanation: A spinlock keeps the waiting thread in a busy loop (spinning) checking the lock repeatedly, which wastes CPU but avoids the overhead of context switching for very short waits.

questions.question: What is priority inversion?
options: When a low-priority process is scheduled before a high-priority one
When a high-priority task is indirectly blocked by a lower-priority task holding a required resource
A scheduling policy that reverses process priority
A hardware interrupt overriding the OS scheduler
questions.explanation: Priority inversion occurs when a medium-priority task preempts a low-priority task holding a resource needed by a high-priority task, effectively blocking the high-priority task.

questions.question: What does TCP stand for?
questions.explanation: TCP stands for Transmission Control Protocol. It provides reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of data between applications.

questions.question: What is an IP address?
options: A physical address burned into a network card
A unique numerical label assigned to each device on a network
A type of network cable standard
questions.explanation: An IP address is a numerical label (e.g., 192.168.1.1) assigned to each device on a network to identify and locate it.

questions.question: What does DNS stand for and what does it do?
options: Data Network Service — transfers files between hosts
Domain Name System — translates domain names to IP addresses
Distributed Node Service — manages network nodes
Dynamic Network Standard — assigns IP addresses dynamically
questions.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (e.g., google.com) into machine-readable IP addresses.

questions.question: What is a router?
options: A device that connects computers within the same local network only
A device that forwards data packets between different networks
A cable that carries network signals
questions.explanation: A router operates at the network layer (Layer 3) and forwards packets between different networks based on IP addresses.

questions.question: What does HTTP stand for?
questions.explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the foundation of data communication on the web, used to transfer HTML and other resources.

questions.question: What is NAT (Network Address Translation) used for?
options: Assigning DNS names to IP addresses
Mapping private IP addresses to a public IP address for internet access
questions.explanation: NAT allows multiple devices on a private network to share a single public IP address by translating private IPs at the router.

questions.question: What is the main difference between TCP and UDP?
options: TCP uses IP addresses; UDP uses MAC addresses
TCP is connection-oriented and reliable; UDP is connectionless and faster but unreliable
TCP is used for video; UDP is used for web browsing
TCP is for IPv4; UDP is for IPv6
questions.explanation: TCP establishes a connection, guarantees delivery, and orders packets. UDP is faster with less overhead but provides no delivery guarantees.

questions.question: What is BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) primarily used for?
options: Routing packets within a single LAN
Exchanging routing information between autonomous systems on the internet
Assigning IP addresses to DHCP clients
Encrypting data using public key infrastructure
questions.explanation: BGP is the routing protocol of the internet, used to exchange routing and reachability information between autonomous systems (AS).

questions.question: What is the sliding window protocol used for in TCP?
options: Flow and congestion control by limiting unacknowledged data in flight
questions.explanation: TCP's sliding window controls how many bytes a sender can have outstanding (unacknowledged) at once, enabling efficient flow and congestion control.

questions.question: What type of routing protocol is OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)?
questions.explanation: OSPF is a link-state routing protocol. Each router maintains a map of the entire network topology and uses Dijkstra's algorithm to compute shortest paths.

questions.question: What is a VLAN (Virtual Local Area Network)?
options: A physical separation of network hardware
A logical network segmentation that groups devices regardless of physical location
A VPN tunnel between two network nodes
questions.explanation: A VLAN logically segments a physical network into multiple virtual networks, improving security and reducing broadcast domains without requiring separate hardware.

questions.question: What is the purpose of TLS/SSL in HTTPS?
options: To compress web content for faster transfer
To cache web pages close to the user
To provide encrypted and authenticated communication over HTTP
To load balance traffic across web servers
questions.explanation: HTTPS uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) to encrypt data in transit and authenticate the server, protecting against eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks.

questions.question: What does HTML stand for?
questions.explanation: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard language for creating and structuring content on the web.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of CSS?
options: Styling and controlling the visual presentation of HTML elements
questions.explanation: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) describes how HTML elements should be displayed — controlling layout, colors, fonts, and spacing.

questions.question: What is the DOM (Document Object Model)?
options: A database for web application data
A programming language for web servers
A tree-structured representation of an HTML document that can be manipulated with JavaScript
A network protocol for web communication
questions.explanation: The DOM is a programming interface representing an HTML document as a tree of objects, allowing JavaScript to read, modify, and update the page dynamically.

questions.question: What does a REST API primarily use to communicate?

questions.question: Which HTTP status code indicates that a resource was not found?
questions.explanation: HTTP 404 (Not Found) means the server cannot locate the requested resource at the given URL.

questions.question: What does HTML stand for (Practice 1)?
questions.explanation: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard language for creating and structuring content on the web. (Practice variant.)

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of CSS (Practice 2)?
options: Styling and controlling the visual presentation of HTML elements
questions.explanation: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) describes how HTML elements should be displayed — controlling layout, colors, fonts, and spacing. (Practice variant.)

questions.question: What is CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)?
options: A method for compressing HTTP responses
A browser security mechanism that controls which cross-origin requests are allowed
A CSS layout system for cross-browser support
A caching strategy for static assets
questions.explanation: CORS is a browser security feature that restricts JavaScript from making requests to a different origin (domain/port/protocol) unless the server explicitly allows it via response headers.

questions.question: What is the key difference between localStorage and a cookie?
options: localStorage is server-side; cookies are client-side
localStorage has no expiry and is not sent in HTTP requests; cookies are sent with every request and can have expiry
Cookies store more data than localStorage
localStorage is encrypted; cookies are not
questions.explanation: localStorage persists data in the browser with no automatic expiry and never sends it to the server. Cookies are automatically included in HTTP requests and can have expiry dates.

questions.question: What is React's Virtual DOM?
options: A shadow copy of the database
An in-memory representation of the real DOM used to compute minimal updates
A special DOM for mobile browsers
questions.explanation: React maintains a Virtual DOM — a lightweight copy of the real DOM. When state changes, React diffs the virtual and real DOM and applies only the minimal necessary updates.

questions.question: What does a CDN (Content Delivery Network) do?
options: Stores user databases across the world
Distributes content from geographically closer edge servers to reduce latency
questions.explanation: A CDN caches and delivers static content (images, scripts, stylesheets) from edge servers geographically close to the user, reducing load times and server load.

questions.question: What is the event loop in JavaScript responsible for?
options: Processing the callback queue to enable non-blocking asynchronous execution
questions.explanation: The JavaScript event loop continuously checks the call stack and callback queue, moving callbacks to the stack when it is empty, enabling asynchronous behavior in a single-threaded language.

questions.question: What is the key difference between Server-Side Rendering (SSR) and Client-Side Rendering (CSR)?
options: SSR uses React; CSR uses Angular
SSR generates the full HTML on the server per request; CSR builds the page in the browser using JavaScript
SSR is faster for SPAs; CSR is faster for static sites
SSR requires a CDN; CSR does not
questions.explanation: SSR renders HTML on the server and sends it to the client, improving initial load and SEO. CSR sends a minimal HTML shell and renders content in the browser via JavaScript.

questions.question: What is a JWT (JSON Web Token) and how is it structured?
options: An encrypted database token split into key and value
A base64url-encoded token with three parts: header, payload, and signature separated by dots
An OAuth 2.0 access token stored in localStorage always
questions.explanation: A JWT consists of three base64url-encoded parts separated by dots: header (algorithm), payload (claims), and signature. It enables stateless authentication.

questions.question: What is WebSocket and how does it differ from HTTP?
options: WebSocket is a CDN protocol; HTTP is for APIs
WebSocket is request-response; HTTP is full-duplex
WebSocket provides a persistent full-duplex channel over a single TCP connection; HTTP is request-response
questions.explanation: WebSocket establishes a persistent connection allowing bidirectional real-time communication. Unlike HTTP where the client always initiates, either side can send messages at any time.

questions.question: What is GraphQL and how does it differ from REST?
options: GraphQL is a graph database query language; REST is for relational databases
GraphQL allows clients to request exactly the data they need in a single query; REST has fixed endpoints returning fixed structures
GraphQL uses WebSockets; REST uses HTTP
GraphQL is only for mobile apps; REST is for web apps
questions.explanation: GraphQL lets clients specify exactly what data is needed in a single request, eliminating over-fetching and under-fetching. REST serves fixed data shapes from multiple endpoints.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a service worker in a web application?
options: To act as a network proxy enabling offline support, push notifications, and background sync
To handle user authentication on the server
questions.explanation: A service worker runs in the background as a scriptable network proxy, enabling Progressive Web Apps (PWA) features like offline caching, background sync, and push notifications.

questions.question: What does IaaS stand for?
questions.explanation: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) provides virtualized computing resources like VMs, storage, and networking over the internet (e.g., AWS EC2, Azure VMs).

questions.question: What does SaaS stand for?
questions.explanation: SaaS (Software as a Service) delivers complete software applications over the internet. Users access them via browser without managing the underlying infrastructure (e.g., Gmail, Salesforce).

questions.question: Which of the following is a major cloud computing platform?
questions.explanation: Amazon Web Services (AWS) is one of the leading cloud computing platforms offering over 200 services including compute, storage, databases, and more.

questions.question: What does PaaS stand for?
questions.explanation: PaaS (Platform as a Service) provides a complete development and deployment environment in the cloud, letting developers build apps without managing servers or runtime (e.g., Heroku, Google App Engine).

questions.question: What is cloud storage?
options: RAM memory in a data center
Storing data on local SSDs of a virtual machine
Storing data on remote servers accessed and managed over the internet
A type of encrypted hard drive
questions.explanation: Cloud storage allows data to be stored on remote servers maintained by cloud providers and accessed from anywhere over the internet (e.g., Amazon S3, Google Drive).

questions.question: What is cloud computing?
options: Using a computer only when it is raining
Delivering computing services (servers, storage, databases, networking) over the internet
A type of local network inside a home
A way to install software without updates
questions.explanation: Cloud computing provides on-demand access to IT resources over the internet, typically with pay-as-you-go pricing and rapid scalability.

questions.question: Which of these best describes a public cloud?
options: Cloud infrastructure dedicated to a single company only
Cloud services delivered over the internet by a provider to many customers
A cloud network that never connects to the internet
A data center located in a private house
questions.explanation: Public cloud services (like AWS, Azure, GCP) are offered by providers to multiple customers over the internet.

questions.question: Which of these best describes a private cloud?
options: Cloud services used by anyone for free
Infrastructure dedicated to a single organization
A cloud that runs only on mobile devices
questions.explanation: A private cloud is dedicated to one organization, offering more control and customization, and can be hosted on-premises or by a provider.

questions.question: What is a hybrid cloud?
options: A cloud that mixes two programming languages
A combination of public cloud and private/on-premises infrastructure
A cloud that only supports hybrid cars
A cloud that uses only containers
questions.explanation: Hybrid cloud combines private/on-premises resources with public cloud services, enabling workloads to move between them.

questions.question: What is an availability zone (AZ) in cloud computing?
options: A billing plan that is always free
A separate data center location within a region designed for high availability
questions.explanation: Availability zones are isolated locations within a region that help improve fault tolerance and availability by spreading resources.

questions.question: What is a cloud region?
options: A geographic area where a cloud provider has multiple data centers
questions.explanation: A region is a geographic area (e.g., West Europe) that contains multiple data centers to host cloud services.

questions.question: What is virtualization?
options: Turning off a server to save power
Creating virtual versions of computing resources like servers and storage
A method for writing faster code
questions.explanation: Virtualization allows multiple virtual machines to run on one physical server by sharing hardware resources through a hypervisor.

questions.question: What is a virtual machine (VM)?
options: A physical computer you carry in a bag
A software-based computer that runs an operating system on virtualized hardware
questions.explanation: A VM is an emulated computer that runs its own OS and applications, sharing underlying physical hardware with other VMs.

questions.question: What is a container in cloud computing?
options: A physical shipping container used in data centers
A lightweight package that includes an app and its dependencies, sharing the host OS kernel
A storage device like an SSD
questions.explanation: Containers (e.g., Docker) package applications with their dependencies so they run consistently across environments, often more lightweight than VMs.

questions.question: What does 'pay-as-you-go' mean in cloud pricing?
options: You pay a fixed price regardless of usage
You pay only for the resources you actually use
You pay only once per year
questions.explanation: Pay-as-you-go pricing bills based on actual consumption (compute time, storage used, data transfer), helping avoid large upfront costs.

questions.question: What is an SLA (Service Level Agreement)?
options: A contract that defines expected service availability and support terms
A programming language for cloud apps
questions.explanation: An SLA defines service guarantees (like uptime) and what happens if the provider fails to meet them.

questions.question: What is multi-tenancy in cloud services?
options: Running one app on only one server
Multiple customers sharing the same underlying infrastructure while remaining isolated
A feature that requires multiple passwords
A method to split a file into many parts
questions.explanation: Multi-tenancy means multiple customers use shared infrastructure, with logical isolation to keep data and workloads separate.

questions.question: What is object storage (e.g., S3-style storage)?
options: Storing data as objects with metadata in buckets
Storing data only on USB drives
Storing data only in tables and rows
questions.explanation: Object storage stores data as objects (files) in buckets and is commonly used for backups, media, and large unstructured data.

questions.question: What is a managed service in the cloud?
options: A service where you manage all servers and patches yourself
A service where the provider handles maintenance like patching and backups
A service that works only on weekends
questions.explanation: Managed services reduce operational work by letting the provider handle tasks like patching, scaling, and high availability (depending on the service).

questions.question: Why do companies use cloud computing?
options: For benefits like scalability, cost efficiency, and faster deployment
Because it prevents all security risks
Because it eliminates the need for software
questions.explanation: Cloud computing helps organizations scale quickly, reduce upfront hardware costs, and deploy services faster using managed platforms.

questions.question: What is auto-scaling in cloud computing?
options: Automatically adjusting the number of compute resources based on demand
A tool for auto-deploying code to production
Automatically scaling prices based on usage
questions.explanation: Auto-scaling automatically adds or removes compute instances based on current load, ensuring performance during peak times and cost savings during low traffic.

questions.question: What is the primary function of a load balancer?
options: Distributing incoming network traffic across multiple servers
questions.explanation: A load balancer distributes incoming requests across multiple backend servers, improving availability, reliability, and preventing any single server from being overwhelmed.

questions.question: What is serverless computing?
options: A model where developers write and deploy code without managing servers; the cloud provider handles infrastructure
Hosting code on a colleague's local machine
Running code on client browsers only
questions.explanation: Serverless computing (e.g., AWS Lambda) lets developers run code in response to events without provisioning or managing servers. You pay only for execution time.

questions.question: What is a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)?
options: A virtual machine with private CPU allocation
A logically isolated section of a cloud provider's network where you launch resources
A VPN connection between two cloud regions
questions.explanation: A VPC provides an isolated virtual network in the cloud. You control the IP address range, subnets, routing tables, and security settings for your resources.

questions.question: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling?
options: Horizontal scales databases; vertical scales web servers
Horizontal adds more instances; vertical increases the size/power of existing instances
Vertical is cheaper; horizontal is more reliable
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling (scaling out) adds more instances to distribute load. Vertical scaling (scaling up) increases the CPU, RAM, or storage of an existing instance.

questions.question: What is Identity and Access Management (IAM) used for in the cloud?
options: To control who can access resources and what actions they can perform
questions.explanation: IAM defines users, roles, and policies to manage authentication and authorization for cloud resources.

questions.question: What does 'least privilege' mean in cloud security?
options: Granting only the minimum permissions needed to perform a task
questions.explanation: Least privilege reduces risk by limiting access to only what is necessary, minimizing the blast radius of mistakes or compromised accounts.

questions.question: What is a snapshot in cloud computing?
options: A point-in-time copy of a disk or database for backup/restore
A tool to automatically scale CPU
questions.explanation: Snapshots capture data at a specific point in time and are commonly used for backups, cloning, or disaster recovery.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a NAT gateway/service in a cloud network?
options: To allow private resources to access the internet without being directly reachable from it
questions.explanation: NAT allows outbound internet access from private subnets while keeping inbound connections blocked unless explicitly allowed.

questions.question: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?
options: Manually clicking in a cloud console to create resources
Defining infrastructure using code/templates so it can be versioned and automated
questions.explanation: IaC tools (like Terraform, CloudFormation, Bicep) let you provision and manage infrastructure consistently and repeatably.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Layer 4 and Layer 7 load balancer?
options: Layer 4 routes based on IP/port; Layer 7 routes based on HTTP details like host/path
Layer 7 is for databases only; Layer 4 is for storage only
Layer 4 can only be used on-premises
questions.explanation: Layer 4 works at the transport layer (TCP/UDP). Layer 7 understands application protocols like HTTP and can do advanced routing.

questions.question: What is TLS termination?
options: Decrypting HTTPS traffic at a load balancer or gateway before forwarding to backend services
A method to terminate virtual machines
questions.explanation: TLS termination offloads encryption/decryption to an edge component (like a load balancer), simplifying backend services and improving performance.

questions.question: What does RPO (Recovery Point Objective) describe?
options: How quickly a system must be restored
The maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time
The maximum number of users allowed
The number of servers in a cluster
questions.explanation: RPO is about how much data you can afford to lose (e.g., last 15 minutes). It influences backup and replication strategy.

questions.question: What does RTO (Recovery Time Objective) describe?
options: How much data you can lose
How long it can take to restore service after an outage
How many regions a provider has
How often you rotate encryption keys
questions.explanation: RTO is about downtime tolerance—how quickly the service must be back online after a disruption.

questions.question: What is a message queue commonly used for in cloud architectures?
options: To decouple services by buffering messages between producers and consumers
questions.explanation: Queues (like SQS, Service Bus, Pub/Sub) help build resilient systems by smoothing traffic spikes and decoupling components.

questions.question: What is a reserved capacity/commitment discount in cloud pricing?
options: Paying more to get slower performance
Committing to use resources for a term in exchange for lower cost
A way to avoid paying for storage
A free trial for serverless only
questions.explanation: Many providers offer discounts when you commit to a certain usage level or term (e.g., 1–3 years), reducing overall cost.

questions.question: What is a storage lifecycle policy?
options: A policy that deletes all data immediately
Rules that automatically transition or delete data based on age or access patterns
A policy to increase CPU automatically
A policy to force password changes
questions.explanation: Lifecycle policies help manage cost by moving older data to cheaper tiers (or expiring it) automatically.

questions.question: What is multi-region deployment used for?
options: To improve availability and reduce latency by running services in multiple geographic regions
To eliminate the need for backups
questions.explanation: Deploying across regions can improve resilience against regional outages and provide faster response times to global users.

questions.question: What is a managed Kubernetes service (e.g., EKS/AKS/GKE)?
options: A service where you manually maintain the Kubernetes control plane
A service where the cloud provider manages the Kubernetes control plane components
A tool for writing Kubernetes YAML automatically
questions.explanation: Managed Kubernetes services offload control plane management (upgrades, availability), letting teams focus on workloads and cluster configuration.

questions.question: What is cloud monitoring/observability used for?
options: To collect metrics, logs, and traces to understand system health and performance
questions.explanation: Observability helps detect issues, troubleshoot outages, and understand performance using logs, metrics, and distributed traces.

questions.question: What is a Kubernetes Pod?
options: A virtual machine in a Kubernetes cluster
The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes, containing one or more containers sharing network and storage
questions.explanation: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It encapsulates one or more containers that share the same network namespace (IP address) and storage volumes.

questions.question: What is a CDN edge location?
options: The physical location of your origin server
A geographically distributed data center that caches content close to end users
The edge of a virtual network subnet
A type of content compression node
questions.explanation: CDN edge locations are globally distributed points of presence (PoPs) that cache your content. Users are served from the nearest edge location, reducing latency significantly.

questions.question: What is cloud-native architecture?
options: Running legacy monolithic applications on cloud VMs
Designing and building applications specifically to leverage cloud characteristics like elasticity, microservices, and managed services
Using only one cloud provider for all services
Building applications without any vendor-specific dependencies
questions.explanation: Cloud-native architecture designs applications to exploit cloud capabilities: microservices, containers, dynamic orchestration, and continuous delivery for scalability and resilience.

questions.question: What is a service mesh?
options: A virtual network connecting microservices in a Kubernetes cluster
An infrastructure layer handling service-to-service communication with features like load balancing, observability, and security
A mesh Wi-Fi system for cloud data centers
questions.explanation: A service mesh (e.g., Istio, Linkerd) is a dedicated infrastructure layer that manages communication between microservices, handling retries, circuit breaking, mTLS, and distributed tracing.

questions.question: What is blue-green deployment?
options: Using two different cloud providers simultaneously
A deployment strategy that maintains two identical environments and switches traffic between them for zero-downtime releases
Deploying blue (stable) features first and green (experimental) features second
A color-coded alert system for deployment failures
questions.explanation: Blue-green deployment keeps two identical production environments. The new version is deployed to the idle (green) environment, and traffic is switched from blue to green, enabling instant rollback.

questions.question: What is a canary deployment?
options: Deploying to all users at once
Releasing a new version to a small subset of users/traffic before a full rollout
A deployment that requires no monitoring
questions.explanation: Canary deployments reduce risk by gradually shifting traffic to the new version while monitoring metrics and rolling back if problems occur.

questions.question: What is the CAP theorem in distributed systems?
options: A principle stating a distributed system can only guarantee two of Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance
A rule that all systems must be consistent
questions.explanation: CAP highlights trade-offs in distributed systems, especially under network partitions. Systems balance consistency and availability depending on requirements.

questions.question: What is eventual consistency?
options: Data is always immediately consistent everywhere
Data may be temporarily inconsistent but will converge to a consistent state over time
Consistency only applies to file storage
questions.explanation: In eventually consistent systems, replicas may not update instantly, but they converge, improving availability and performance in distributed environments.

questions.question: What is an ingress controller in Kubernetes?
options: A tool to build Docker images
A component that manages external access to services, typically HTTP/HTTPS routing
questions.explanation: Ingress controllers (like NGINX, Traefik) implement Ingress rules to route external traffic to services inside a cluster.

questions.question: What is a Kubernetes Operator?
options: A person who runs commands in kubectl
A controller that extends Kubernetes to manage complex applications using custom resources
questions.explanation: Operators encode operational knowledge to automate tasks like backups, upgrades, and failover using Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs).

questions.question: What is the sidecar pattern in cloud-native applications?
options: Running an app on a car dashboard
Deploying a helper container alongside the main container to provide extra capabilities
Using only one container per cluster
A method to store data in side tables
questions.explanation: A sidecar container runs in the same Pod as the main app container to add features like proxying, logging, or configuration without changing app code.

questions.question: What is mTLS (mutual TLS) in a service mesh?
options: A method where both client and server authenticate each other using certificates
A way to store secrets in plain text
questions.explanation: mTLS authenticates both ends of a connection and encrypts traffic, improving service-to-service security within microservice environments.

questions.question: What is a circuit breaker pattern used for?
options: To physically protect servers from power surges
To prevent cascading failures by stopping calls to an unhealthy dependency
questions.explanation: Circuit breakers detect failures and temporarily block requests to failing services, allowing recovery and preventing a failure cascade.

questions.question: What is distributed tracing?
options: A way to trace network cables in a data center
Tracking a single request across multiple services using trace IDs and spans
A method to delete logs faster
questions.explanation: Distributed tracing follows requests across microservices, helping identify bottlenecks and failures through correlated spans and trace IDs.

questions.question: What is the difference between a control plane and a data plane?
options: Control plane handles user interfaces; data plane handles billing
Control plane manages configuration/orchestration; data plane carries actual application traffic
Data plane manages encryption keys only
questions.explanation: The control plane makes decisions and manages state (e.g., scheduling, routing policies). The data plane forwards traffic and executes those decisions.

questions.question: What is idempotency and why is it important for cloud APIs?
options: A way to encrypt API calls
Repeating the same request multiple times produces the same result, helping safely retry operations
A method to speed up CPU
questions.explanation: Idempotent operations can be retried safely (e.g., after timeouts) without creating duplicate resources, which is critical in distributed systems.

questions.question: What is a Pod Disruption Budget (PDB) in Kubernetes?
options: A limit on container image size
A policy that controls how many pods can be voluntarily disrupted during maintenance
A tool to choose Pod IP addresses
questions.explanation: PDBs help maintain availability by limiting voluntary disruptions (like node drains) so a minimum number of pods stay running.

questions.question: What is a zero trust security model?
options: Trusting all internal network traffic by default
Assuming no implicit trust and continuously verifying identity and access
Using only one password for all services
questions.explanation: Zero trust means 'never trust, always verify'—authenticate, authorize, and encrypt regardless of network location.

questions.question: What is data egress in cloud computing?
options: Data leaving a cloud provider's network, often billed separately
questions.explanation: Egress is outbound data transfer from the cloud. Providers often charge for it, so architecture choices (CDNs, caching) can impact cost.

questions.question: What is a Kubernetes namespace commonly used for?
options: To logically separate and organize resources (teams, environments) within a cluster
questions.explanation: Namespaces help isolate and organize resources in a cluster, often used for multi-tenant separation, environments (dev/prod), and access control.

questions.question: What does CI/CD stand for?
questions.explanation: CI/CD stands for Continuous Integration (automatically building and testing code on every commit) and Continuous Delivery/Deployment (automatically releasing validated code).

questions.question: What is Docker primarily used for?
options: Containerizing applications and their dependencies for consistent execution across environments
questions.explanation: Docker packages an application and its dependencies into a lightweight container that runs consistently on any system that has Docker installed.

questions.question: What is Git?
options: A distributed version control system for tracking changes in source code
questions.explanation: Git is a distributed version control system that tracks changes to files, enables collaboration, and allows reverting to previous versions of code.

questions.question: What is a container in computing?
options: A virtual machine with its own OS kernel
A lightweight, isolated environment that packages an application with its dependencies
A physical server dedicated to one application
questions.explanation: Containers package an application and its dependencies together, sharing the host OS kernel. They are more lightweight and faster to start than virtual machines.

questions.question: What does DevOps primarily focus on?
options: Bridging development and operations to enable faster, more reliable software delivery
questions.explanation: DevOps is a culture and set of practices that combines software development and IT operations to shorten the development lifecycle and enable continuous, high-quality delivery.

questions.question: What is Kubernetes used for?
options: Automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications
questions.explanation: Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration platform that automates deploying, scaling, load balancing, and managing containers across clusters of machines.

questions.question: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?
options: Writing code that runs inside infrastructure hardware
Managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable configuration files instead of manual processes
A GUI tool for configuring servers
Running application code on physical servers
questions.explanation: IaC (e.g., Terraform, AWS CloudFormation) lets you define and provision infrastructure in version-controlled configuration files, enabling repeatable and auditable deployments.

questions.question: What is a Dockerfile?
options: A log file generated when a container crashes
A YAML configuration file for Kubernetes
A script containing instructions to build a Docker image layer by layer
A network configuration file for containers
questions.explanation: A Dockerfile is a text file with step-by-step instructions (FROM, RUN, COPY, CMD, etc.) that Docker uses to build a container image.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a CI pipeline?
options: Automatically build, test, and validate code changes on every commit
Deploy code to production automatically without testing
questions.explanation: A CI pipeline automatically triggers build and test processes on every code commit, catching integration bugs early and ensuring code quality before merging.

questions.question: What is Terraform?
options: A container runtime alternative to Docker
An open-source Infrastructure as Code tool for provisioning cloud resources
questions.explanation: Terraform by HashiCorp is an IaC tool that lets you define cloud and on-premises infrastructure in declarative configuration files and manages the lifecycle of those resources.

questions.question: What is a Kubernetes DaemonSet?
options: A set of Kubernetes secrets for daemon processes
A controller that ensures a specific pod runs on every (or selected) node in the cluster
A background service that monitors pod health
A Kubernetes resource for running cron jobs
questions.explanation: A DaemonSet ensures that a copy of a Pod runs on all (or selected) nodes. Common use cases include log collectors, monitoring agents, and network plugins.

questions.question: What is canary deployment?
options: Deploying a full release to all users simultaneously
Rolling back a failed deployment immediately
Gradually routing a small percentage of traffic to a new version to limit blast radius before full rollout
Running two versions in parallel permanently
questions.explanation: Canary deployment releases a new version to a small subset of users first. If no issues arise, it is progressively rolled out to all users, minimizing risk.

questions.question: What is GitOps?
options: Using Git only for code review processes
A practice where Git repositories are the single source of truth for infrastructure and application deployments, with automated reconciliation
An automated tool for Git repository backups
The process of optimizing Git repository performance
questions.explanation: GitOps uses Git pull requests as the mechanism for all infrastructure and deployment changes. Automated agents continuously reconcile the live system state with the desired state in Git.

questions.question: What does observability in DevOps refer to?
options: The ability to watch live video streams from data center cameras
The practice of code review observing team collaboration
The ability to infer the internal state of a system from its external outputs — logs, metrics, and traces
Auditing user access logs for compliance
questions.explanation: Observability is the ability to understand what is happening inside a system based on its outputs. The three pillars are logs (events), metrics (measurements), and distributed traces (request flows).

questions.question: What is a Helm chart in the context of Kubernetes?
options: A graph showing cluster resource utilization
A Kubernetes YAML template for a single pod
A package that bundles Kubernetes manifests, templates, and metadata for deploying applications
questions.explanation: Helm is the package manager for Kubernetes. A Helm chart is a collection of files that describe a set of Kubernetes resources, making it easy to deploy and manage complex applications.
